message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
This is a dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (do NOT flag as bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only, nothing leaves the box), no metered API (subscription OAuth only), engine never imports adapters/datasets (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends are lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; prefer findings you can back with a concrete repro/PoC against the venv. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects (not style/nits). Each finding needs a real file path and, where possible, a line.

You are independent VERIFIER #1 of 2. A prior adversarial assessor reported this production-readiness finding:

TITLE: No supply-chain vulnerability scanning (pip-audit) in CI for a security/privacy-focused production engine
DIMENSION: ops, packaging & supply chain
CLAIMED SEVERITY: MEDIUM
FILE: .github/workflows/ci.yml 8
DESCRIPTION: Neither pyproject.toml nor ci.yml contains any dependency-vulnerability scanning step (`grep -n 'pip-audit\|safety\|bandit'` returns nothing). The project pulls a large, fast-moving transitive surface through its extras — verified by resolving the `observability` extra alone, which installs 150+ packages including cryptography, aiohttp, Flask/Werkzeug, requests, protobuf, grpcio, openai, anthropic, boto3, and SQLAlchemy. With only floor+major-cap ranges (e.g. `cryptography` unconstrained as a transitive, `aiohttp` transitive, `requests` transitive), a clean install on any future day can pull a transitive version with a freshly-disclosed CVE and CI will never notice. For a tool that brands itself for security & privacy data and is being hardened for production, the absence of an advisory-database check (and of a lockfile / constraints file for the heavy lanes) is a genuine supply-chain gap rather than a style preference.
SUGGESTED FIX: Add a CI job (or step in the integration lane) that runs `pip-audit` against the installed environment, failing on known vulnerabilities (with an allowlist file for accepted/unfixable advisories). Consider also committing a constraints/lock file (e.g. `pip-compile` output or `uv.lock`) for the heavy extras so installs are reproducible and audited, while keeping pyproject ranges permissive for library consumers.

Independently determine whether this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the actual code; attempt a concrete PoC against the venv if applicable. Set is_real=false if it is a false positive, already-handled, or merely a documented/justified design choice given the hard constraints. Recalibrate severity. Provide your evidence (what you read/ran).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	name: ci
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    branches: [main]
6	  pull_request:
7	
8	jobs:
9	  # Dependency-free core: the engine must lint, type-check, and test with NO extras installed
10	  # (optional backends are import-guarded; integration tests skip). This guards ADR-0006.
11	  core:
12	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
13	    strategy:
14	      matrix:
15	        python-version: ["3.11", "3.12", "3.13", "3.14"]
16	    steps:
17	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
18	      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
19	        with:
20	          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
21	      - name: Install (no extras)
22	        run: |
23	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
24	          pip install -e ".[dev]"
25	      - name: Lint (ruff)
26	        run: ruff check src tests
27	      - name: Format check (ruff)
28	        run: ruff format --check src tests
29	      - name: Type-check (mypy)
30	        run: mypy
31	      - name: Test (pytest)
32	        run: pytest -q
33	
34	  # Extras lane: install the real backends and run the integration suite so the backend code
35	  # paths actually execute, and mypy type-checks against the REAL (not ignored) library types.
36	  # Service-dependent tests (ollama/qdrant/postgres) skip when the service is absent in CI; the
37	  # extra-only tests (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, numpy primitives, methodology
38	  # e2e) run for real.
39	  integration:
40	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
41	    services:
42	      postgres:
43	        image: postgres:16
44	        env:
45	          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
46	          POSTGRES_DB: assay
47	        ports: ["5432:5432"]
48	        options: >-
49	          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U postgres"
50	          --health-interval 5s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 10
51	      qdrant:
52	        image: qdrant/qdrant:v1.12.4
53	        ports: ["6333:6333"]
54	    env:
55	      # loopback host keeps the data-sovereignty guard satisfied; creds are redacted on error
56	      ASSAY_TEST_POSTGRES_URL: postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/assay
57	    steps:
58	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
59	      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
60	        with:
61	          python-version: "3.12"
62	      - name: Install (all extras)
63	        run: |
64	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
65	          pip install -e ".[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]"
66	      - name: Type-check against real backend types (mypy)
67	        run: mypy
68	      # Real backends now run: langgraph graph + interrupt/resume, mlflow lifecycle, OTel,
69	      # numpy primitives, the T3 drain/classification (synthetic SDK, no metered call), AND —
70	      # via the service containers — the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store.
71	      # Ollama and the metered frontier tier are not in CI and skip honestly.
72	      - name: Integration + unit tests with coverage
73	        run: pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing
74

content.text: No lock or constraints files. Let me read pyproject.toml to check the dependency ranges and extras, and check SECURITY.md and the .githooks for any scanning.

content.content:
1	[build-system]
2	requires = ["hatchling"]
3	build-backend = "hatchling.build"
4	
5	[project]
6	name = "assay-engine"
7	version = "0.0.1"
8	description = "Dataset-agnostic engine for rigorous, reproducible empirical ML/NLP on security & privacy data."
9	readme = "README.md"
10	requires-python = ">=3.11,<3.15"
11	license = "Apache-2.0"
12	license-files = ["LICENSE"]
13	authors = [{ name = "[REDACTED]" }]
14	keywords = ["empirical", "reproducibility", "methodology", "security", "privacy", "ml", "nlp"]
15	classifiers = [
16	    "Development Status :: 4 - Beta",
17	    "Intended Audience :: Science/Research",
18	    "Intended Audience :: Developers",
19	    "Operating System :: OS Independent",
20	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3",
21	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.11",
22	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.12",
23	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.13",
24	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.14",
25	    "Topic :: Scientific/Engineering",
26	    "Topic :: Security",
27	    "Typing :: Typed",
28	]
29	# The engine core is dependency-free; heavy backends are optional extras (lazy-imported,
30	# ADR-0006). The composed runner and methodology work with no extras installed.
31	dependencies = []
32	
33	[project.urls]
34	Homepage = "https://github.com/[REDACTED]/hc-assay"
35	Repository = "https://github.com/[REDACTED]/hc-assay"
36	Issues = "https://github.com/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/issues"
37	
38	[project.optional-dependencies]
39	dev = [
40	    "pytest>=8",
41	    "ruff>=0.6",
42	    "mypy>=1.11",
43	]
44	# Heavy backends for the reasoning seam — installed only where real LLM calls run.
45	# The engine imports and unit-tests (mocking the backends) without these.
46	reasoning = [
47	    "langchain-ollama>=1.1,<2",
48	    "claude-agent-sdk>=0.2.101,<0.3",
49	    "anyio>=4,<5",
50	]
51	observability = [
52	    "opentelemetry-sdk>=1.25,<2",
53	    "opentelemetry-exporter-otlp>=1.25,<2",
54	    "arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18",
55	    "openinference-instrumentation-langchain>=0.1.40,<1",
56	    "mlflow>=3.1,<4",
57	]
58	persistence = [
59	    "langgraph>=1.2,<2",  # core — provides MemorySaver (declared, not left transitive)
60	    "langgraph-checkpoint-postgres>=3.1,<4",
61	    "psycopg[binary,pool]>=3.2,<4",
62	    "qdrant-client>=1.9,<2",
63	]
64	orchestration = [
65	    "langgraph>=1.2,<2",
66	]
67	baseline = [
68	    "numpy>=1.26,<3",  # vectorized similarity at corpus scale (pure-Python fallback otherwise)
69	]
70	# Every backend in one shot (for a full local install). dev tooling stays separate.
71	all = [
72	    "assay-engine[reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]",
73	]
74	
75	[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]
76	packages = ["src/assay_engine"]
77	
78	[tool.pytest.ini_options]
79	testpaths = ["tests"]
80	pythonpath = ["src"]
81	
82	[tool.ruff]
83	line-length = 100
84	src = ["src", "tests"]
85	
86	[tool.mypy]
87	python_version = "3.11"
88	packages = ["assay_engine"]
89	strict = true
90	
91	# Optional reasoning/observability backends are not installed in the type-check env; their
92	# imports are lazy and guarded at runtime, so treat them as untyped third-party modules.
93	[[tool.mypy.overrides]]
94	module = [
95	    "langchain_ollama.*",
96	    "claude_agent_sdk.*",
97	    "anyio.*",
98	    "opentelemetry.*",
99	    "phoenix.*",
100	    "mlflow.*",
101	    "openinference.*",
102	    "langgraph.*",
103	    "qdrant_client.*",
104	    "psycopg.*",
105	    "psycopg_pool.*",
106	    "numpy.*",
107	]
108	ignore_missing_imports = true
109

1	# Security Policy
2	
3	## Reporting a vulnerability
4	
5	Please report security issues **privately** — do not open a public issue for a vulnerability.
6	
7	Use GitHub's private vulnerability reporting ("Report a vulnerability" under the repository's
8	**Security** tab), or open a regular issue asking for a private channel without disclosing
9	details.
10	
11	Please include: what the issue is, how to reproduce it, and the impact you foresee. We aim to
12	acknowledge a report within a few days.
13	
14	## Scope notes
15	
16	hc-assay is local-first and data-sovereign by design (ADR-0003): the engine routes execution
17	through a loopback-only / on-box path and scrubs metered API credentials before any subprocess
18	call. Findings that weaken these guarantees — data leaving the box, a metered credential
19	reaching a subprocess, a firewall/pre-registration bypass, or a forgeable provenance trail — are
20	in scope and especially welcome.
21	
22	The engine's honest-scope limits are documented (e.g. in-process trust for the unkeyed
23	provenance chain, signature-level rather than frame-isolation firewalls); a report that defeats a
24	guarantee *within* its stated scope is a valid finding.
25

content.content:
total 8
drwxr-xr-x   3 [REDACTED]  staff   96 Jun 16 15:53 .
drwxr-xr-x  24 [REDACTED]  staff  768 Jun 16 15:54 ..
-rw-r--r--   1 [REDACTED]  staff  540 Jun 16 15:53 pre-commit
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Provenance: snapshot the active Claude session transcript(s) into transcripts/ on every commit
# so each commit carries an auditable record of the agent interactions/effort behind it.
# Best-effort — never block a commit.
set -u
ROOT="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel 2>/dev/null)" || exit 0
PY="$ROOT/.venv/bin/python"
[ -x "$PY" ] || PY="$(command -v python3 || true)"
[ -n "$PY" ] || exit 0
"$PY" "$ROOT/scripts/capture_transcripts.py" 2>/dev/null || true
git add -- "$ROOT/transcripts" 2>/dev/null || true
exit 0

content.text: The finding is factually confirmed. Let me verify the claim about the transitive surface and that no allowlist/CI scanning exists anywhere. Let me also confirm the data-sovereignty constraint doesn't somehow obviate this.

content.text:
All claims verified concretely:

- The full transitive surface the assessor named is present: cryptography 48.0.1, aiohttp 3.14.1, requests, Flask/Werkzeug, protobuf, grpcio, openai, anthropic, boto3, SQLAlchemy, urllib3, Jinja2, Pillow (217 packages total with extras).
- No `pip-audit`/`safety`/`bandit` anywhere in CI (ci.yml only does ruff/mypy/pytest), pyproject, dev extras, git hooks, or docs.
- No lock/constraints file; deps use floor+major-cap ranges and many security-sensitive packages (cryptography, aiohttp, requests, urllib3) are unconstrained transitives.

Now let me calibrate against the hard constraints. The data-sovereignty constraint (loopback-only, nothing leaves the box) does not eliminate supply-chain risk — a vulnerable dependency executes on the box where the sensitive security/privacy data lives, which is precisely the threat model. SECURITY.md actively brands the project for security/privacy work. So the constraints reinforce rather than excuse the gap.

On severity: this is a real, undisputed gap, but it's a defense-in-depth/process gap rather than an active exploitable defect in the engine's own code. CI absence of advisory scanning is a genuine production-readiness ops gap. MEDIUM is the right calibration — it doesn't break functionality and the engine core has zero hard dependencies, but for a tool hardened for production handling sensitive data, missing supply-chain scanning on a 217-package extras surface is a legitimate medium-severity ops/supply-chain finding.

input.reasoning:
Confirmed as a genuine production-readiness ops/supply-chain gap, not a false positive or a justified design choice.

What I verified:
1. CI (.github/workflows/ci.yml) has two jobs (core, integration) and runs only ruff, ruff format, mypy, and pytest. There is NO dependency-vulnerability scanning step. (Read full 74-line file.)
2. `grep -rniE 'pip-audit|safety|bandit|cyclonedx|osv|trivy|grype'` across .github/, pyproject.toml, docs/, scripts/, CONTRIBUTING.md returns nothing relevant (only unrelated "auditable provenance" mentions). The dev extra (pyproject lines 39-43) is just pytest/ruff/mypy.
3. No lockfile or constraints file exists anywhere in the repo (searched for *.lock, constraints*.txt, requirements*.txt). Dependencies use floor+major-cap ranges (e.g. cryptography is not even directly declared — it is an unconstrained transitive; aiohttp/requests/urllib3 likewise transitive).
4. The heavy extras surface is real and large: the venv with extras installed has 217 packages, and I confirmed every security-sensitive package the assessor named is actually present: cryptography 48.0.1, aiohttp 3.14.1, requests 2.34.2, Flask 3.1.3, Werkzeug 3.1.8, protobuf 6.33.6, grpcio 1.81.1, openai 2.41.1, anthropic 0.109.2, boto3 1.43.30, SQLAlchemy 2.0.51, urllib3 2.7.0, Jinja2 3.1.6, Pillow 12.2.0.

Hard-constraint check: The local-first/data-sovereign constraint does NOT excuse this. A CVE in a transitive dependency executes on the very box that holds the sensitive security/privacy data — that is the exact asset the project protects. SECURITY.md brands the project as security/privacy-focused and invites supply-chain-adjacent findings. None of the documented constraints (loopback-only, no metered API, engine-never-imports-adapters, lazy heavy backends) address advisory-database scanning. So the gap is real and the constraints actually reinforce it rather than waive it.

Severity calibration: MEDIUM is correct. It is a defense-in-depth/process gap, not an active exploitable bug in the engine's own code, and the engine core itself has zero hard dependencies (the 217-package surface only appears when extras are installed). But for a tool being hardened for production that handles sensitive data through a large, fast-moving transitive surface with no lockfile and no advisory check in CI, this is a legitimate medium-severity supply-chain/ops finding. Not HIGH (no demonstrated active vulnerability today; core is dependency-free), not LOW (it is more than a nit for a security-branded production engine).
input.evidence: Read /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml (full): jobs core+integration run only ruff/ruff format/mypy/pytest; no scan step. Read /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/pyproject.toml (full): dev extra = pytest>=8, ruff>=0.6, mypy>=1.11 only; backend extras use floor+major-cap ranges; cryptography/aiohttp/requests/urllib3 not directly declared (transitive, unconstrained). Read /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/SECURITY.md: branded local-first/data-sovereign security tool, no scanning policy. grep for pip-audit/safety/bandit/osv/trivy across .github/, pyproject, docs/, scripts/, CONTRIBUTING.md = no matches. No lockfile/constraints/requirements file in repo. `/.venv/bin/pip list | wc -l` = 217 packages with extras. `pip list | grep` confirmed present: cryptography 48.0.1, aiohttp 3.14.1, requests 2.34.2, Flask 3.1.3, Werkzeug 3.1.8, protobuf 6.33.6, grpcio 1.81.1, openai 2.41.1, anthropic 0.109.2, boto3 1.43.30, SQLAlchemy 2.0.51, urllib3 2.7.0, Jinja2 3.1.6, Pillow 12.2.0. `pip list | grep -iE 'pip-audit|safety|bandit'` returns nothing (no scanner installed). .githooks/pre-commit only snapshots transcripts; no scanning.

content.text: Verified as a true production-readiness finding at MEDIUM severity. Key file: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml (no scan step) and /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/pyproject.toml (217-package extras surface, unconstrained security-sensitive transitives, no lockfile). The data-sovereignty hard constraint reinforces rather than excuses the gap.
